(Dutch pronunciation: [[ˈeːrədiˌvizi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch"); "Honour Division" or "Premier Division"), also known as **VriendenLoterij Eredivisie** for sponsorship reasons, is a professional [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") league in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") and the highest level of the [Dutch football league system](/wiki/Dutch_football_league_system "Dutch football league system"). The league was founded in 1956, two years after the start of professional football in the Netherlands. As of the 2024–25 season, it is [ranked](/wiki/UEFA_coefficient#Men's_association_coefficient "UEFA coefficient") the sixth-best league in Europe by [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Dutch association football league

This article is about the top men's football league in the Netherlands. For the top women's football league in the Netherlands, see [Eredivisie (women)](/wiki/Eredivisie_(women) "Eredivisie (women)").

For other uses, see [Eredivisie (disambiguation)](/wiki/Eredivisie_(disambiguation) "Eredivisie (disambiguation)").

The **Eredivisie** (Dutch pronunciation: [[ˈeːrədiˌvizi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch"); "Honour Division" or "Premier Division"), also known as **VriendenLoterij Eredivisie** for sponsorship reasons, is a professional [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") league in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") and the highest level of the [Dutch football league system](/wiki/Dutch_football_league_system "Dutch football league system"). The league was founded in 1956, two years after the start of professional football in the Netherlands. As of the 2024–25 season, it is [ranked](/wiki/UEFA_coefficient#Men's_association_coefficient "UEFA coefficient") the sixth-best league in Europe by [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The Eredivisie consists of 18 clubs. Each club meets every other club twice during the season, once at home and once away. At the end of each season, the two clubs at the bottom are [relegated](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation") to the second level of the Dutch league system, the *[Eerste Divisie](/wiki/Eerste_Divisie "Eerste Divisie")* (First Division), while the champion and runner-up of the Eerste Divisie are automatically promoted to the Eredivisie. The club finishing third from the bottom of the Eredivisie goes to separate promotion/relegation play-offs with six high-placed clubs from the Eerste Divisie.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The winner of the Eredivisie claims the [Dutch national championship](/wiki/List_of_Dutch_football_champions "List of Dutch football champions"). [Ajax](/wiki/AFC_Ajax "AFC Ajax") have won the most titles with 36. [PSV Eindhoven](/wiki/PSV_Eindhoven "PSV Eindhoven") are next with 26, and [Feyenoord](/wiki/Feyenoord "Feyenoord") follow with 16. Since 1965, these three clubs have won all but three Eredivisie titles (the 1981 and 2009 titles went to [AZ](/wiki/AZ_Alkmaar "AZ Alkmaar") and [Twente](/wiki/FC_Twente "FC Twente") won in 2010). Ajax, PSV, and Feyenoord are known as the "[Big Three](/wiki/Big_Three_(Netherlands) "Big Three (Netherlands)")" or "Traditional Top Three" of Dutch football. They are the only clubs in their current form to have never been relegated out of the Eredivisie. A fourth club, [Utrecht](/wiki/FC_Utrecht "FC Utrecht"), is the product of a 1970 merger between three of that city's clubs, one of which, [VV DOS](/wiki/VV_DOS "VV DOS"), had also never been relegated out of the Eredivisie.

From 1990 to 1999, the official name of the league was PTT Telecompetitie (after the sponsor, PTT Telecom), which was changed to [KPN](/wiki/KPN "KPN") Telecompetitie (because PTT Telecom changed its name to KPN Telecom) in 1999 and to KPN Eredivisie in 2000. From 2002 to 2005, the league was called the Holland Casino Eredivisie. Since the 2005–06 season, the league has been sponsored by the *Vriendenloterij* (lottery), but for legal reasons its name could not be attached to the league (the Dutch government was against the name, because the Eredivisie would, after Holland Casino's sponsorship, yet again be sponsored by a company providing games of chance). From the 2025-2026 season the eredivisie will be rebranded to *VriendenLoterij Eredivisie*.

In August 2012, it was made public that tycoon [Rupert Murdoch](/wiki/Rupert_Murdoch "Rupert Murdoch") had secured the rights to the Eredivisie for 12 years at the expense of one billion euros, beginning in the 2013–14 season.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Within this deal, the five largest Eredivisie clubs were to receive five million euros per year.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In 2020, the Eredivisie was abandoned due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eredivisie&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

From the foundation of the Dutch national football championship in 1898 until 1954, the title was decided through play-offs by a handful of clubs who had previously won their regional league.[[5]](#cite_note-rsssf-5) The competition was purely an amateur one; the [Royal Dutch Football Association](/wiki/Royal_Dutch_Football_Association "Royal Dutch Football Association") (KNVB) rejected any form of payment and suspended players who were caught receiving salary or transfer fees.[[6]](#cite_note-vakq-6) The call for professional football grew in the early fifties after many national team members left to play abroad in search for financial benefits.[[7]](#cite_note-timerime-7) The KNVB would usually suspend these players, preventing them from appearing for the [Dutch national team](/wiki/Netherlands_national_football_team "Netherlands national football team"). After the [North Sea flood of 1953](/wiki/North_Sea_flood_of_1953 "North Sea flood of 1953"), the Dutch players abroad (mainly playing in the [French league](/wiki/Ligue_1 "Ligue 1")) organised a charity match against the [France national team](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team") in Paris. The match was boycotted by the KNVB, but after the assembled Dutch players defeated the French (2–1), the Dutch public witnessed the heights that could be achieved through professional football.[[8]](#cite_note-8) To serve the growing interest, a dissident professional football association (the NBVB) and league were founded for the [1954–55 season](/wiki/1954%E2%80%9355_Netherlands_Football_League_Championship "1954–55 Netherlands Football League Championship").[[9]](#cite_note-rsssf2-9) On 3 July 1954, the KNVB met with a group of concerned amateur club chairmen, who feared the best players would join the professional teams. The meeting, dubbed the *slaapkamerconferentie* ('bedroom conference'), led to the Association reluctantly accepting semi-professionalism.[[6]](#cite_note-vakq-6)

Meanwhile, both the KNVB and the NBVB started their separate competition. The first professional football match was contested between [Alkmaar](/wiki/AZ_Alkmaar "AZ Alkmaar") and [Venlo](/wiki/VVV-Venlo "VVV-Venlo").[[6]](#cite_note-vakq-6) The leagues went on for eleven rounds, before a merger was negotiated between the two federations in November. Both leagues were cancelled and a new, combined competition emerged immediately. [De Graafschap](/wiki/De_Graafschap "De Graafschap"), Amsterdam, Alkmaar and [Fortuna '54](/wiki/Fortuna_Sittard "Fortuna Sittard") from the NBVB were accepted to the new league. Other clubs merged, which led to new names like [Rapid J.C.](/wiki/Roda_JC_Kerkrade "Roda JC Kerkrade"), Holland Sport and Roda Sport. The first (semi-)professional league was won by [Willem II](/wiki/Willem_II_(football_club) "Willem II (football club)").[[10]](#cite_note-10) For the [1956–57 season](/wiki/1956-57_Eredivisie "1956-57 Eredivisie"), the KNVB abandoned the regional league system. The Eredivisie was founded, in which the eighteen best clubs nationwide directly played for the league title without play-offs. The inaugural members of the Eredivisie in 1956 were [Ajax](/wiki/AFC_Ajax "AFC Ajax"), [BVC](/wiki/AFC_DWS "AFC DWS"), [BVV](/wiki/FC_Den_Bosch "FC Den Bosch"), [DOS](/wiki/VV_DOS "VV DOS"), [EVV](/wiki/FC_Eindhoven "FC Eindhoven"), [Elinkwijk](/wiki/USV_Elinkwijk "USV Elinkwijk"), [SC Enschede](/wiki/Sportclub_Enschede "Sportclub Enschede"), [Feijenoord](/wiki/Feyenoord "Feyenoord"), Fortuna '54, [GVAV](/wiki/FC_Groningen "FC Groningen"), [MVV](/wiki/MVV_Maastricht "MVV Maastricht"), [NAC](/wiki/NAC_Breda "NAC Breda"), [NOAD](/wiki/TSV_NOAD "TSV NOAD"), [PSV](/wiki/PSV_Eindhoven "PSV Eindhoven"), Rapid J.C., [Sparta](/wiki/Sparta_Rotterdam "Sparta Rotterdam"), [VVV '03](/wiki/VVV-Venlo "VVV-Venlo") and Willem II.[[11]](#cite_note-rsssf57-11) Ajax was the first team to claim the title that season.[[11]](#cite_note-rsssf57-11) Below is a complete record of how many teams played in each season throughout the league's history;

* 18 clubs: 1956–1962
* 16 clubs: 1962–1966
* 18 clubs: 1966–present

Since the beginning of the league, there have been three clubs with an attendance much higher than the others: Ajax, PSV and [Feyenoord](/wiki/Feyenoord "Feyenoord"). Clubs like [Heerenveen](/wiki/SC_Heerenveen "SC Heerenveen"), [Utrecht](/wiki/FC_Utrecht "FC Utrecht") and [Groningen](/wiki/FC_Groningen "FC Groningen") also have fairly large fanbases. The regular season average league attendance was just over 7,000 in 1990, but this figure has risen sharply over the years thanks to the opening of new stadiums and the expansion of existing ones nationwide. Average attendance for the 2018–19 season was 18,010, with Ajax having the largest (52,987) and [Excelsior](/wiki/S.B.V._Excelsior "S.B.V. Excelsior") having the smallest (4,223). Ajax's figures however differ from those provided by the [Johan Cruyff Arena](/wiki/Johan_Cruyff_Arena "Johan Cruyff Arena"), since the club counts all tickets sold instead of the number of people going through the turnstiles.
